context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i'm kinda lost on the city case study data table rn, idk what stuff i'm even supposed to look up first.
exchanges.tutor: That feeling is totally normal at the start! Before we dive in — have you already chosen a city to study? That's the foundation everything else builds on.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. Rather than listing out what to do, I should use a guiding question to help them engage with the assignment themselves. I'll point them back to the assignment instructions and ask a simple orienting question to get them started without giving them a roadmap.

exchanges.student: yeah but tbh i'm still confused even w the city picked. do i just find random stats like population/gdp/climate zone or is there some bigger point?
exchanges.tutor: Good question — and it's worth thinking about carefully. The assignment actually tells you why this data matters. Can you take another look at the Overview section and tell me what it says the purpose of understanding existing conditions is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a good conceptual question about the 'why' behind the data collection. Rather than explaining it for them, I should guide them to find the answer themselves by pointing them back to the assignment's stated purpose. This is a good Socratic moment to help them connect the data to the broader goal.

exchanges.student: i read it but ngl it all kinda blurs together. can u just explain what this assignment is basically trying to get me to understand?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but working through it yourself is part of the learning. Try this: in the Overview, there are two bullet points explaining what this data will give you insight into. What do those two bullets say, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the assignment for them, which would bypass their own engagement with the material. I should resist doing that and instead break it down with a targeted Socratic question that nudges them to re-read a specific part. I'll keep it short and direct them to a specific sentence in the overview rather than summarizing it myself.

exchanges.student: umm maybe like what the city is like now and what climate problems it has? and maybe where money should go but idk how demographics fits that tbh
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with that summary. Now think about this: who tends to be most affected when a city faces a climate disaster like flooding or extreme heat? Does that help you see why demographic data might matter for deciding where resources should go?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid first attempt at paraphrasing the overview and is now asking a genuinely good conceptual question about how demographics connects to climate investment priorities. This is a great Socratic moment — rather than explaining the connection, I should ask a question that helps them reason it out themselves.

exchanges.student: kinda, but i still feel like i need the whole thing explained fr. like how do population, race, income, area, gdp, and climate zone all connect?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to lay it all out for you — that's your job! But let's take it one step at a time. You already said lower-income or vulnerable groups might be hit harder by climate impacts. So what might a city's income per capita tell you that raw population numbers alone wouldn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing for a full explanation, which I should not provide. They've already shown they can reason through individual pieces — I should hold the line and use a Socratic question to help them build the connections one at a time rather than all at once. I'll pick one concrete pairing to get them thinking rather than overwhelming them or giving in to their request.

exchanges.student: maybe if ppl have less money they have less ability to prepare or recover? but then i'm stuck again on why gdp is separate, idk if i'm overthinking it
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid insight about income and resilience. On GDP vs. income per capita — think about it this way: one measures the total economic output of the whole city, the other measures how that wealth is distributed per person. What might the gap between those two numbers tell you about a city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good insight about income and vulnerability. Their question about GDP vs. income per capita is a legitimate and productive one — not overthinking. I should affirm the good reasoning briefly and ask a targeted question to help them distinguish the two concepts themselves.

exchanges.student: idk maybe that a city can be rich overall but ppl in it still aren't? i'm still not seeing how to actually write the table tho, like what goes in each part and how much detail is enough
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the GDP point — that gap is meaningful. For the format question, the assignment actually mentions there's an example of a completed data table provided. Have you looked at that yet? It should answer most of your formatting questions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the GDP vs. income per capita distinction — I should briefly affirm that. Now they're shifting to a procedural question about how to fill in the table. The assignment mentions an example completed table as a resource, which I should point them to rather than describing format myself. I'll keep it short and redirect them to that resource.

exchanges.student: not yet tbh. if i open it, what am i supposed to copy from the example vs make specific to my city?
exchanges.tutor: Open it first, then come back with what you're seeing. You'll be in a much better position to answer that question yourself once you've looked at it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable question but wants me to pre-interpret the example for them before they've even looked at it. I should redirect them to actually open it first — they can't meaningfully engage with this question without having seen it. I'll keep it brief and direct.

exchanges.student: ok but i'm worried i'll still blank lol. should i just match the columns/categories from the example and only change the actual data to my city?
exchanges.tutor: You're overthinking it before you've even seen it. Go look at the example, then come back. You've shown good reasoning so far — trust that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is anticipating confusion before even looking at the example, which is a bit of an avoidance pattern. Their instinct about the structure is reasonable, but I shouldn't confirm or deny it — I should encourage them to just look at the example and trust themselves to figure it out. I'll be brief and a little firm.

exchanges.student: ugh yeah maybe. i just want a super simple idea of what "done enough" looks like bc rn it all feels vague.
exchanges.tutor: I get the frustration, but the best way to clear the vagueness is to just start. The assignment tells you the minimum categories required. Once you've filled those in for your city, you'll have a much clearer sense of whether something feels missing. Go look at the example and take a first pass — then bring what you have here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and looking for reassurance about completion standards. This is understandable but I should not define 'done enough' for them — that would undermine their engagement with the assignment. I can acknowledge the frustration briefly and point them back to the assignment's own language about what's required, then nudge them to just start.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently applied Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, redirecting the student back to the assignment text and guiding them to derive insights themselves rather than providing answers. No near-submission-ready content was given, no student text was rewritten, and key ideas were kept student-led. Scaffolding was appropriate and built on student progress. Responses were concise and tone was supportive without being evaluative.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave direct answers or submission-ready content. Instead, it repeatedly pointed the student back to the assignment overview, asked guiding questions (e.g., about who is most affected by climate disasters, what GDP vs. income per capita reveals), and withheld formatting details until the student engaged with the example. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor acknowledged correct student reasoning (e.g., 'That's a solid insight about income and resilience,' 'Exactly right on the GDP point') and built on it progressively. No diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level guidance throughout, e.g., directing the student to read the assignment text themselves and trust their own reasoning, rather than just commenting on correctness. 2.1: Each turn advanced the conversation without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student stayed on-task and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment. 3.1: Responses were appropriately brief and interactive. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without implying formal grading.
